top_categories.workout_context: 205 miles. Your legs will beg to quit after 120. This trains the power output you need from mile 130 onward.
26,247ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 205 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before June.
205 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 26,247ft of climbing on paved Alpine mountain passes through three does to your power file on race day.
Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo's paved Alpine mountain passes through three forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 205 miles.
128ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
At Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
26,247ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo. Train it until it feels boring.
Tempo is Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo's race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 205 miles on paved Alpine mountain passes through three. Train it until it's automatic.

race_overlay.nutrition: Ultra-distance fueling for 205 miles: 80–100g carbs/hour from mile 1 — don’t wait until you’re hungry. Practice your exact race-day nutrition in every long training ride. Carry backup calories. 205 miles burns 8,000–12,000+ calories — you cannot replace them all, but you must try.
race_overlay.altitude: Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo with 26,247ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%. Pre-dawn start at altitude: 5-10C in Megeve at 5:00 AM; Great St. Bernard summit (2,469m): 2-5C with wind chill below zero.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, VO2max, and Endurance to prepare you for 205 miles of paved alpine mountain passes through three countries in Les Saisies, Haute-Savoie, France.
